 Course Outline


                                                                       7
▪ Mean-variance (MV) portfolio problem                          week
    ▪ Setting up the problem and understanding the key inputs

▪ Forecasting expected returns and volatilities
    ▪ Predicting return on equities and bonds
    ▪ Predicting volatilities
    ▪ Long-term Capital Market Assumptions (LTCMAs)
    ▪ Using third party LTCMA forecasts


                                                                           Week
▪ Solving the mean-variance (MV) portfolio problem for a multi-asset allocation
    ▪ Finding the optimal asset mix (optimal weights)
    ▪ Studying optimal portfolio performance


                                                 FINS 3640
 Portfolio Management
• https://www.blackrock.com/au/intermediaries/products/254711/blackrock-blk-global-allocation-fund-aust-
  d-fund

• « Portfolio management consists in investing your assets in a way that seeks to grow your financial wealth over time, or to reach
  specific financial objectives to carry out future projects. »


• « Since it can be done over different time horizons and must take into account your cash flow needs and the financial impact of
  your decisions, it requires expertise, experience and objectivity. »


• « You already know that your investments must be diversified – by sector, asset classes, country and management styles – and
  rebalanced over time to reduce the risk associated with market fluctuations. Yet, they must also keep in line with any change that
  may take place in your life. »


• « Therefore, the management approach you choose must seek capital accumulation while minimizing the volatility of your
  portfolio. »




                                                            FINS 3640                                                                  3
 Steps in the PF Management Process
• Purpose of this process: to achieve the return objective (E(R)) while considering the risk tolerance
  (individual or institutional) and respecting the constraints of the investors


• The main steps in the portfolio management process
1. Planning
    • Identification of customer needs
    • Definition of an investment policy: investment objectives, constraints
2. Execution
    • Determination of asset allocation
    • Choice of strategies (active, passive)
    • Construction of the PF
3. Feedback
    • Monitoring / rebalancing of the PF
    • Performance evaluation / reports


See Appendix A for a more detailed description of each step.

 Investment Policy Statement (IPS)
• An investment policy statement (IPS) is a document drafted between a portfolio manager and a client that
  outlines general rules for the manager.


• Specific information on matters such as asset allocation, risk tolerance and liquidity requirements are
  included in an investment policy statement.


• Objectives of the IPS: realistic choices and objectives taking into account the different constraints of the
  investor
     • Promotes a long-term management discipline: avoid panic or overconfidence
     • Allows better evaluation of the portfolio manager's performance (in particular by comparing it with a
       benchmark)
     • Protects investors from inappropriate asset management or unethical behavior on the part of managers




                                                  FINS 3640                                                  6
 Investment Policy Statement
     Investment objectives
     • Performance objective
     • Tolerance / risk aversion
     Constraints
     • Liquidity constraints
     • Investment horizon
     • Regulatory constraints
     • Tax considerations
     • Specific considerations
                                   FINS 3640   7
 The Standard MV Portfolio Problem                                                                  Steffen
• The standard assumption is that investors like higher mean (expected returns) and dislike higher variance.


• Under this assumption the class of potentially optimal portfolios for such investors are therefore those with the
  greatest expected return for a given level of variance and, reciprocally, the smallest variance for a given expected
  return.


• Such portfolios are termed mean-variance efficient.


• This is the basic principle of portfolio construction of Markowitz (1952).


• This principle defines the industry practices of numerous pension funds and mutual funds.


• Today, we are doing a deep dive into the required steps for implementing an asset mix, that is, finding the optimal
  weights of a portfolio constituted of various financial assets.

                                                       FINS 3640                                                         8

• We first need to find the minimum-variance portfolios in the absence of a riskless asset.
• The minimum-variance portfolio with expected return μ𝑝 , is the solution 𝑤𝑖 for all asset i to:

• Forecasts for expected returns and volatility are at the really core of portfolio construction and the design of
  investment strategies including
     • Volatility-managed portfolios,
     • Long-short portfolio based on expected return forecasts,
     • etc…


• We now discuss the modeling and forecasting of returns and volatility.

   Forecasting expected returns and volatilities
• The quality of the forecasts critically depend on the horizon.
    • For expected returns, it is usually easier to forecast them at longer horizons than at shorter ones.
          •   Easier to forecast next year returns, or next 5 years returns than next month/week/day returns.
     • For volatility, it is the opposite. Forecasting at shorter horizon is usually easier because volatility is persistent
       over time (i.e., low (high) volatility today is likely to be followed by low (high) volatility tomorrow).

• There are many ways to forecast return and volatility:
    • Aggregating market participants expectations about return and volatility.
    • Analyzing macro-level data (GDP growth, inflation, employment, immigration, etc…).
    • Use of models which require to estimate parameters (in-sample v.s. out-of-sample model performance).
          •   Different models work well for different forecast horizons.


• Realized/historical v.s. expected returns and volatility
    • Knowing the past does not mean knowing the future (in-sample v.s. out-of-sample model performance)
    • Changes in regulations, unexpected macro-events (war, pandemic, country defaulting on sovereign debts, etc…)
       are not always foreseeable.

                                                                       FINS 3640                                               11
 Forecasting using OLS
• Various methodology can be used to forecast returns or volatilities. One approach is to adopt predictive OLS
  regressions.

• The idea of predictive regression is relatively simple. The goal is to predict future return/volatility using variables
  informative about expected returns/volatility.

• The variable to be predicted corresponds to the return or volatility next period: next month return/volatility, next
  year return/volatility, …

• The explanatory variables used to predict future return/volatility is lagged: They correspond to the variables that
  would be observed or that could be constructed one period before the return/volatility to be predicted.
     •   If a variable helps predict future return/volatility (i.e., is statistically significant), we say that this variable has pred ictive power.
     •   If a variable has predictive power, then, an investor could use it to predict return/volatility (i.e., obtain an estimate of expected return/volatility)
         and trade accordingly on the signal obtained.


• Predictive regression take the form:                                       it
                                                                   𝑦𝑡 = α + β 𝑥𝑡−1 + u𝑡


  where 𝑦𝑡 is period-t return or volatility and 𝑥𝑡−1 is the predictor(s) computed one period before.

                                                                                                                             19
 Python Application 1: Predicting SPX returns
Context:
• Jan Martin, a famous finance academics from LSE, has shown that the VIX-squared is a good proxy for stock
  market expected returns.
• The VIX is a volatility index constructed from derivatives. This index is available in real-time just like the S&P 500.


Python Application:
• Step 1 - We download SPX and VIX data for the last 20 years.
• Step 2 - We calculate 1-month future SPX returns and create a DataFrame combining VIX at time t and future SPX
  returns for the next month recorded as of time t.
• Step 3 - Using a 10-year fixed rolling window, we estimate the regression model with VIX-squared predicting
                                         2
  future SPX returns: 𝑟𝑆𝑃,𝑡 = α + β 𝑉𝐼𝑋𝑡−1  + u𝑡 . We construct each month the model forecast for next month S&P
  500 and stored out-of-sample predictions.
• Step 4 - Out-of-Sample R-squared: We compute the out-of-sample R-squared based on out-of-sample
  predictions of the model and compared them to a 5-year rolling mean S&P500 return benchmark model.


• See Application 1 - Predicting SPX returns.ipynb for the solution.

                                                                                                                     20
 Forecasting using OLS
In- and out-of-sample performance:
• The more explanatory variables a model has, the better its explanatory power in-sample in general.
• But a good in-sample performance does not always translate to good out-of-sample performance.
• The predictive power of models is thus generally assessed out-of-sample (i.e., outside of the time-period used to
  estimate it).
     •   Just like the way an investor would implement a model to trade on it: estimate the model using data available up to the tradi ng date and then
         use the model predictions to trade on it out-of-sample.
Choice of explanatory variables:
• Finding variables 𝑥𝑡 that robustly predict returns is notoriously hard.
     •   VIX (Volatility index of SPX); Earning price ratio (E/P); dividend payout ratio (D/E); Dividend-price ratio (D/P); net equity expansion (new equity
         issued/aggregate market capitalization); percent equity issuing (ratio of equity issue to total equity and debt issuance); Book-to-market ratio
         (B/M); Term spread (TMS) - Difference between the long-term government yield and the Treasury bill rate; Default yield spread (DFY) - Difference

                                                                         inflation
         between BAA-and AAA-rated corporate bond yields; etc …
• Finding variables 𝑥𝑡 that robustly predict corporate bond returns is also hard, but bond returns are easier to predict
  than stock returns.
     •   On top of stock return predictors: Aggregate leverage ratio (LEV) – average ratio of book value of debt to the sum of book debt and market
         equity value; 5-year government bond yield; Stock index returns; proportion of junk bond issuers to total bond issuers (IQ); pro portion of long-
         term debt issued relative to total debt issued

• Finding variables 𝑥𝑡 that robustly predict volatility is much easier than for predicting returns
     •   Lagged measures of variance: last week realized variance, last month realized variance, last quarter realized variance, last year realized variance.

Goyal and Welch (2008):
• Use an extensive list of predictors for market equity returns.
                                                                                                       youth later
• Estimate predictive regressions 𝑟𝑡 = α+ β 𝑥𝑡−1 + u𝑡 where 𝑥𝑡−1 is a given predictor and 𝑟𝑡 is month-t market return.
• Use an out-of-sample expanding rolling window approach.
• Their analysis is based on monthly returns. It starts on December 1927 and ends in December 2004.
• To construct their first forecast, they estimate the coefficients using an initial estimation of 20 years of monthly data (from
  December 1927 to December 1947).

     Forecasting volatilities: HAR model
• Corsi (2009) proposes an OLS predictive model for variance: HAR. The model uses lagged variance measures to predict
  future variance.
                                                                                         𝑑
• The original model uses next day realized variance as the variable to be predicted (𝑅𝑉𝑡+1 ).
• As predictor, the model uses (lagged) one-day realized variance (𝑅𝑉𝑡𝑑 ), weekly realized variance (𝑅𝑉𝑡𝑤 ) and monthly
  realized variance (𝑅𝑉𝑡𝑚 ). All these measures are computed using up time t information.

      fixed and analyzed the quality of model fit out-of-sample without updating the parameters.

   Forecasting volatilities: HAR model
• In-sample, the model obtains a R-squared of 70.7% for S&P 500 daily variance.
• Out-of-sample, the model R-squared remains high around 62.7% when predicting S&P 500 variance 2 weeks
  ahead.
• Variances and volatilities are MUCH easier to forecast than returns!!

     Forecasting volatilities: GARCH model
• The GARCH (Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity) family of models captures important features
  of returns data such as: volatility clustering and persistence, mean reversion in volatility (high period of volatility are
  usually followed by period of lower volatility and vice-versa).

• The model is usually defined as follow:
                                                r𝑡 = μ + 𝜎𝑡 u𝑡 with u𝑡 ~N 0,1

                                      𝜎𝑡 is the volatility of r𝑡 and is known as of time t
